transcript.text: Hey, Ric.

Definitely different than it would have been if I got engaged in my 20s.
We're going to, like, Oregon and, like, in this huge, you know, nice cabin out in nature, watching a bunch of college football, chasing waterfalls.
So I think in my 20s, I probably would have been in Vegas or something regrettable like that.
It's funny how things change, but honestly, that sounds so much better.
There's something chill, you know?
Yeah, I mean, I'm sure I'll return hungover.
There's no doubt about it.
Just getting some quality time with the boys this go around.
Man, that is awesome.
Oh, I'm going to bring.
Oh, yeah, sorry.
I'm just going bring in Meshach and Bryce.
Cool.
Yeah.
I think there was some issue with the link.

Gotcha.
No worries.
Hey, Bryce.
Hey, Meshach.
Hey, how's it going?
Hey, everyone.
Yeah, it's going great.
I was just catching up with Rick.
It's my last day in the office before I go on my bachelor party tomorrow.
So pretty excited.
Oh, congrats.
Where are headed?
Going to Oregon.
Yeah, we're going to do it out in nature.
And I've never been there before.
So the Columbia River Gorge and all those trails and waterfalls, in addition to a lot of college football, is the agenda.
That sounds great.
Very cool.
I'm up in Seattle, so not too far from it.
Okay, nice.
Yeah, last time I was out there, I went to Olympic National Park.
But We'll
So I'm doing a good job, doing a good job with the nature for sure.
Yeah, yeah.
Excellent.
Good.
Thanks for sharing some stuff.
Yeah, absolutely.
Marcel should be joining us shortly here.
And I had a chance to catch up with Rick and Meshach last week.
I guess as we're waiting for Marcel, we'd love to just kind of catch up on where you guys are at internally.
Maybe do like an intro from you, Bryce, and I can give an intro as well.
And then I'm sure Marcel will hop in by that time.
Great.
So I guess I'll start with my intro, then we can go from there.
I'm Bryce, CMO, been here about four months now.
Interesting ride.
Like we are, we're growing quickly as a company.
And really, it's an opportunity for us to do category creation.
So the company has built a lot of content, but for human absorption and human practitioners, quite frankly.
So as we think about up-leveling our positioning, but also...
So, you know, making it more crawlable.
And, you know, that's sort of an opportunity for us.
I feel like we have a source of strength with long-form content and good, solid kind of depth to it.
But we're only now evaluating how do we get this really going with GEO and the like.
Awesome.
Yeah, that's good.
Hey, everyone.
So sorry.

Cancel your last one right now.

Only five.
Only five, yeah.

It's energizing.
I like days like this.

Unfortunately, I don't get to eat.
I think this thing has been sitting here for like two hours.
Yeah.
At least feel free during this meeting.
That's all good.
Yeah, yeah.
So sorry to jump in, but I think you were just giving a little bit more context, right?
Yeah.
And we can talk a little bit about, Tyler, what you guys did last time and so on.
missed that meeting, but I'm excited to at least be engaging with you guys.
Yeah, absolutely, Bryce.
That overview helps.
And Marcel, I can just do a quick recap of the conversation I had with Rick and Meshach last week to give us good context here.
That sounds good.
Yeah, and by the way, like super familiar with the space as well.
And so it's, and it sounds like you guys have done quite a bit of content already, right?
And so it's like one thing, and happy to kind of jump in and show more like kind of into weeds if you think would be helpful.
But I'll always be curious, like if you kind of had this map.
What would be the thing that is the limiting factor to more growth, more organic growth right now for you all?
Any thoughts there?
Yeah, I would say that one of the things we're doing is having people reframe.
A lot of times in cybersecurity, people think from the defenders, like, how do I understand my estate versus how do adversaries understand the estate?
So one of the things we are trying to do is get enough content out there that it almost flips the mindset to, okay, now I'm going to look through the glasses of the adversary.
Because once you do that, you see all the paths.
And literally what our product does is it tells the adversary how to get to the goods.
And then how can you remediate that?
So there's, I would say, Marcel, the lift for us is absolutely getting people to think differently.
And so how we put that in terms of like actual metrics and so on is another thing.
But at scale, I just absolutely know that I can help.
Yeah, yeah.
I love that.
And I don't know if you guys knew, but like one of my first jobs or like early on in my career, I built a site called SecureIntelligence.com.
Okay.
And it was the first off domain site for IBM.
It just got folded back into IBM.com.
And we had just acquired QRadar and another Israeli company called Trusteer.
And then my job was to consolidate all of that.
And in the process of doing that, I'm like, hey, they have way better content than IBM does.
And why aren't we throwing this in a  IBM block?
And I just spin up another site and just like make this good.
And then I got our research team, XForce, at the time called XForce.
don't know if they're still called that.
They are giving us access to their zero day vulnerability stuff that they had, their POCs.
And then we would do a spin on that.
And then we would hit up, you know, Brian Krabs and others and be like, hey, we have this really cool zero day vulnerability that Microsoft just patched.
Do you want to cover it from a less technical?
And then we'll cover it from a proof of concept perspective.
And then that stuff started getting crazy picked up.
And people started linking to it because it's like primary, you know, data almost, right?
And then the site gained a bunch of authority and then it was kind of like, and then after that, I built a site called techbeacon.com that also covers security.
And then after that, I was at HashiCorp and so one of the products was like very security.
And then now we work with a lot of security companies as well, like SentinelOne, Abnormal Security, and a few others.
So security has been near and dear to my heart for a long time, you know?
Yeah.
Excellent.
So when we, when we start geeking out, you understand what we're...
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
I can't say I'm as technical as I used to be, you know, but I think I can, I understand conceptually and I've talked to, most importantly, a ton of security people.
So understand the nuances of, you know, don't bring your devices to black hat if you don't want to get hacked kind of thing, you know?
Yeah.
Good.
So I, we're trying to also just manage how much of this, like, so...
In our thought leadership, when we put blogs out, it's very technical by design, and we end up getting like 3,000 5,000 views from it.
But it's pretty like SEO oriented, not GEO oriented.
And so we're also trying to figure out like where do we need, how do we need to tweak content?
And then where do we actually need to get it out into third party?
We think our sentiment is good on Reddit and the like, because we're very, very community based.
But quite frankly, we haven't measured it.
So it's that kind of stuff where it's a multi-pronged problem, and you guys are deep in it.
And we're trying to figure out where do we start knocking down the, you know, early opportunities.
So maybe like I can just, I promise it's not, we'll go super deep.
But maybe like I always like to start with like a little, taking a little bit of a step back.
And so give just a second.
I have this like kind of visual, I'm a visual person, you know.
And so like there's a couple of things that we saw in order to, first of all, like scale quality.
And so...
A lot of what's happening right now, especially with AI answers, is that it may be in security slightly less because security people are skeptical in nature.
And so that's a good thing, I guess.

But a lot of people, they're researching things in AI and getting AI answers, and they're almost considering that word of mouth, like an expert is telling them that, even though LLMs don't have a huge gray filter on the quality of the sources they're citing using the answers, right?
That doesn't mean they won't do it eventually.
So it's kind of like almost the equivalent of early days of SEO, where you could just stuff keywords and you call it a day.
And I think for us, we just believe that, like, optimize for quality and credibility with the audience that you actually want to serve, and that will eventually be rewarded.
And there's things you can do today to also get that rewarded right away without actually ever doing anything that breaks the trust with your actual human readers, right?
And so, like, that's fundamentally what we try to do is, like, scale with experts in the loop.

Then trying to work backwards from how experts would work and achieve quality, and then figuring out where can we use AI to scale, not to abstract human in a way, but to do an even deeper job, a better job, and optimize for scale and speed while maintaining or even increasing quality, right?
So that's kind of like the way we kind of think about it.
And for that, like 15 years ago, I would have told you the same thing as this slide, which maybe you consider that a bad thing, but for me, which is like, how do we serve people?
How do we serve the right audience?
How do we become, like, create these resources that they would pay for, but instead we're giving away for free?
And that always comes from, like, a deeper understanding of the audience, right?
Like, it's not just like, hey, you're a security person.
Like, no, no, there's like much, there's like a way deeper way to understand their jobs to be done.
The, you know, like the things that they really, really care about, right?
Like the pain points, what are the things that will cause them to get fired?
Who are the stakeholders they need to do?
You know, what are the work products they have to deliver?
And all of that, right?
And so that's kind of like a little bit of how we approach it.
And then like from that, then we can kind of create this map of topics that they care about, which is also, you know, the map of things we're going to track in LLM visibility.
And it's also the things we need to go execute against, right?
And so like just to give you maybe a little bit of like under the hood of kind of how we work.

And so like, unfortunately, I can't show you like the inside of like companies that are like in the same space as you, just because it's like, it would be a little bit unbecoming of us, you know, even though there's no proprietary data here, right?
Like it's more like editorial artifacts, but what we do is we create artifacts.
So think of the artifacts as, you know, I guess take a step back.
Like we mapped out your audience, understand your business, map out like the kind of content we should be creating.
And then from there, when we get to execution, like we're creating these artifacts that what there are is like really going deeper into understanding.
Your personas, your companies, your products, and things like writing guidelines and things like that.

And then from there, we can create these pipelines that look very different, especially for technical companies, and how we achieve that quality systematically with a human in the loop, an editor in the loop that is, you know, has the context of your company.
But I always like to be transparent on this stuff because, like, with technical spaces, like, we're never going to be more of an expert than your researcher, right?
Like, then you're, you know, a security person that's been in the weeds for 20 years, right?
Like, that's not what we're trying to do.

What we're trying to do is understand and get those inputs and consistently execute against those inputs to be able to scale quality, and then as much as possible, continue to enrich it with your perspective on the space or on the topic, right?
In other words, like, we can't come up with thought leadership out of thin air, because it wouldn't be you, right?
But we can find information.

Research the information, figure out how to communicate that information really well, and understand and process how you see the market, how you see the space, how you see those topics, and match those two, and ensure quality and continue to raise the bar.

And then hopefully, if at that point, it's like, hey, we need to add more of our unique view into things, we can do things that are more interview-based, or even conduct the interviews ourselves, or process transcripts and things like that.
And I can give you more examples, but this is cool just to see how our system works.

What we built under the hood, and you guys are in a technical space, so tell me if this is a little too in the weeds or not, but what we built is a coding agent that then writes these processes in code in a runtime layer.
So the way to kind of think about, like, every one of our workflows is in code, you know, and it's all here in code, right?
Like, our prompts, everything is in code.
And so that allows us to...
Build these agentic workflows and deterministic workflows that do these awesome things.
So, for instance, this one is an agentic researcher supervisor.
And so what it does is generates a research plan.
And what it's doing is ingesting a lot of that context, right?
And then figuring out what tools to use.
And we have several different APIs that we can use, like Exit AI, Perplexity, Parallel, Deep Researcher from OpenAI, and Plot.
And then figuring out, like, what is the success criteria?
And what are the requirements of the kind of sources that would make sense to pull, right?
So, for instance, like, A, look at OWASP only.
Or look at, like, you know, the vulnerability database over here.
Or only consider, you know, third-party blogs from notable security researchers.
Don't use new sources.
Don't do this, right?
Like, and then you can figure out, like, what that success criteria is.
And then what it does is, like, it starts to evaluate that.
And run through that process and generating additional questions, and then compile it all into a research brief.
But the research brief is like super crazy comprehensive, right?
And then an editor that works with you can go in here and use our like assistant that's context or similar to like a cursor-like experience, right?
And shape this content in addition to just doing line edits as well and shaping it, right?
So instead of having to copy and paste this  over to like a plot or something else, right?
And then like continue to do that.
And then the same thing for the drafter and whatnot.
And then we take it all the way to the finish line, right?
Like so if you think about what I was showing you, like you can't get tax advice wrong.
And it is technical in the sense that, you know, the IRS is your source of truth, but there's a lot of technicalities about it, right?
And I think the cool thing about this is like we're taking the same, you know, potential source, which is the IRS version of...
The tax law, right?
And then you're trying to apply to, hey, here's the context of town and what town does.
And by the way, here's the context of a persona or a type of business that I want to show.
And by the way, here's the writing guidelines or how we want to sound and what we want to say and how we want to say it.
And when you bring that all together with an expert in the loop, then you can achieve quality, you know.
But also at the same time, be really personalized, right?
Like, you know, Dr.

Sarah found out otherwise for five years into running her $1.8 million practice as a professional corporation, just go over that, blah, And these are all like things that are, you know, specific to dental examples.
Like if you're running a dental practice and you're thinking about your taxes, you know.
And so I'll shut up here, but one last thing I'll just say that I think is super relevant, especially for technical spaces.
And what we're seeing is like incredible success.
And what it made us realize is like this is almost inset.
In a lot of ways.
And so the day before we published this article, they weren't showing up for how to get AI coding tools to understand our entire code base.
And the day after, they're not only showing up, but it's using the exact language, you know, and then they're showing up in AI mode as well.
And even the way we do that deep research, it's almost like you're researching, then you're inserting your brand into the answer.
And then that answer is high quality, incredible, and becomes part of the answer in AI answers, which sounds kind of crazy to say.
And so what we're seeing is like a lot of the factors that make a huge impact are the things we were already doing.

Like, hey, deep research and good attribution, making sure the answers are comprehensive, but well-formatted, well-structured, that there's authority signals of like, hey, so-and-so said this and how they said it and why they said it and why they're an expert, you know.
And then overall, like your brand reputation, which you already have a good one as well.
Well, that's always possible.
How much?
That's lot.
I just try to get us super humble.
And there's a couple of things running through my mind.
We are very good, not to go too technical, but we are very good at offensive security.
The team came from red teamers, Marcel, so they know how to attack companies.
So our whole brand is like leading edge, get to critical assets, the adversary point of view.
And that's really, really good.
If you were to ask us how do deals get done, deals get done because identity gets in the loop, okay?
And so the identity security team decides.
And by the way, speaking to that persona can be, I don't want to say dumbed down, but at least clarified.
It can be simplified a bit, right?
Yeah.
It can be about Like their IAM teams or like the user access identity and all that.
Okay.
Yeah.
And so I think we're jumping over that audience a bit more.
All right.
No, we are.
And what we're going to be doing is prioritizing our kind of persona flow, content flow for that audience.
So I'll just plug that since you talked about your background.
Yeah.
So there is a, pardon me?
That makes sense.
Essentially, like, if I'm hearing it correctly, like, red teaming and, you know, like, and these kinds of experts, like, they're very unique kind.
Yes.
And one, it's really hard to win their trust.
But two, there's not, like, millions of them out there in the world either, right?
Yeah.
And then, like, it sounds like from a commercial perspective, like, you need to also know how to talk to and resonate with different audiences that might be the budget owners.
Is that maybe a way to think about it?
You nailed it.
And so we bring all kinds of credibility and we don't want to distract from that or lower that or whatever, but we need to put it in the context of that other owner.
So I think what you just described made me think, you know, that could help us accelerate it.
I'm curious, though, you know, we've also...
Seen and heard that LLMs do a lot of third-party sites and crawl a lot of third-party sites.
So how does what you just described help us sort of influence the rest of that as well?
Like where else can we put it content, right?
Yeah, this is coming up more and more.
I think it's a very, there's a lot of things we want to do more of and experiment there.

But the way I personally think about it is like, no matter what, before you go influence the world, you need to have like your content engine running well that is every single day putting out value into the world that best trust with your audience and earns credibility, right?
And so no matter what, it's not a replacement for that ideally, you know?

Because like I was just talking to a company earlier today, Tyler and I, and they were like, no, no, no, we got the content down and I didn't want to say anything because like their traffic has been going down like by 50% in the last six months and I wasn't going to like throw their team under the bus or anything.
But then they're like, no, no, no, I just want help like hitting up.
People with like these third-party crappy sites that mention us and or don't mention us to make sure they mention us.
I'm like, sure, that is like, that is not a bad strategy as long as you have like this engine as well where you're earning the credibility.

And I think like some of the things we are doing is taking some of the content we're creating and then later on repurposing it as an answer and write it to a person's question or, you know, like things like that, right?
And then like there's things we have done with people once we're working with them.
It used to be backlinking where we would create campaigns to reach out to sites that had good content and tell them, hey, like, I noticed this link is broken.
Like, can we help you?
Here's where you can help fix it.
And by the way, we'll make sure to mention you over here.
And by the way, we're doing this for Reddit.
So you definitely want to backlink for Reddit, don't you?

Like, you know, so it's kind of like that kind of thing, you know, but, but, you know, full transparency, I would say, hey, we've gotten so much demand and what the core of what we do that that's like, we're not doing that for a lot of customers.
You know, I just don't want to be like misleading and be like, yeah, yeah, we do that every day.

And, you know, but I think like once you have a good content strategy that's executing, then repurposing or reaching out to someone or asking them to like update the language that they're using hopefully gives you even more credibility.
And then you can kind of work out something where it's like, oh, yeah, we'll cross link you to over there as well.
And like, it's like a give or take versus just spamming a thousand sites and asking them to talk about you, you know?
Makes sense.
Meshach and Rick, I've been talking a lot.
Any questions that you guys have come to mind?
Not, not, not any topics that we, we, cover.
I think I definitely wanted you to get as much context as possible.
We walked through a lot of this and Tyler's conversation last week.
So one thing I did want to talk about is kind of like manage.

So I think I alluded to this to Tyler before, and correct me if I'm wrong, though, like right now, you know, a lot of our technical content has a lot of eyes on kind of like the, like some of our technical experts, researchers, you know, product members.
How do you kind of build out like that kind of like verification flow into some of the content that's being produced?
Because I know, you know, at times, like especially, you know, later in relationships, you know, sometimes that content could be quite a bit.

So I just want to make sure that we are, like, we're pulling in the right folks earlier on so that we can get that trust from our counterparts on that side of business so that we can, you know, move forward in a way that, okay, the marketing team has it.
They're not just putting out, you know, anything.
They're putting out crap.
They're putting out really, really good quality AI-driven content.
So how does that
Flow, work.
Yeah, so I think there's a couple of different things we think about.

So on one hand, there's definitely calibration processes of where we're showing you how the application of how we want to sound will look like and making sure we're aligned and it's based on a lot of the inputs we get.
So then it's like, okay, cool, yes, this sounds right.
Then the other piece is like ingesting as knowledge base and understanding where your source of truth is.
For instance, like Strati, as I have a lot of CMS, like we look at their doc site and we use their doc site and put it into a knowledge base so that we can retrieve it and verify it as well.
And so there's things like this, which is like a fact checker that goes and breaks down the content into chunks and then extracts passages from each chunk.
And then for each passage does research, it can also be based on internal docs or, you know, a doc site or something else.

or the web, or a filter version of the types of sources within the web to verify statements, you know, and then if there's any, like, parts of it that the confidence level that it's not true is high, so it's a minus two to plus two, so, like, if there's anything that's, like, negative, like, it will, it tends to do, like, a pretty good job, but here's one that's, like, negative.

The energy lift you feel after a 10-minute walk is in your brain, blah, blah, blah, and then here it's, like, here's the evidence of why this was, like, off or misattributed, and then it suggests a rewrite, you know.

It's, like, like, really nuanced, you know, and that's kind of, like, what we're doing is, like, making this as accurate as possible, and I think, like, there's, where things don't go right in, like, gaining the trust of technical, like, stakeholders is when they don't know what they're reviewing for, and we haven't done the calibration, and we haven't ingested enough, like, domain expertise, right, like, we haven't done
The deep dive.
So like with technical companies like Sentinel One, we try to do four or five, sometimes even six deep dives with technical experts.
And we try to like narrow it down into like one space to start to make sure we can get to quality before we're scaling it, you know.
And then also understanding the different types of topics.
If you're saying like, what is a, you know, whatever type of vulnerability, like that's the informational and the bar might be different than like a how to within your product.
So we try to stay away from the ones that are like traps where like, even if you hire a security expert, they might even not hit the bar themselves.
You know what I mean?
Like, and so like, let's pick things that we can win, trust early, and then build on that knowledge to go tackle things that are more complex and higher attack or harder to tackle, you know?
Okay.
But that's just like how we've seen, but like it can change, right?
Like let's collaborate and like we're in it together.
It's not like a, we don't have all the answers, I don't think, and this stuff, even if you weren't using AI is not easy to solve, you know?
Yeah.
Well, we
I want to make you three minutes late to your next question or next meeting, but I have one final question.
Do you guys have, are you looking to expand right now?
Like sometimes, you know, you reach capacities left and right.
Are you looking to expand and particularly in cybersecurity?
Yeah, yeah.
So a couple of things.
So one, we do the, I'm sure Tyler mentioned last time, but just to give you context, Bryce, we do this eight-week strategy and execution sprint.
And it's a way for us to just like deliver a ton of value and, and, and kind of set everything in a good motion.
And then from there, it goes into a longer term commitment.
But, you know, so far, knock on wood, we've had a hundred percent conversion rate on those.
And so like, but we do capacity constrain ourself on purpose.
So we only do two kickoffs a week to, because there's a dedicated team to each engagement.
And it takes a lot of context for them to dive deep into understanding your business.
You can't just like have one meeting and pretend like, you know, everything there is to know about your space.
Right.
Um.
And so that is really like the intentional bottleneck on the business that we put.
And so we're normally four to six weeks out booked.
But because those eight weeks is the only thing you're committing to unless you decide to continue, then like it makes it kind of like a no-brainer for people.
And it's kind of like almost like a loss leader for us because we know we can prove ourselves in those eight weeks.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Cool model.
Good.
Yeah.
That's great.
And we are definitely expanding because we're in the middle of raising our Series B.
All right.
Good.
And so.
Every deal matters.
Every deal matters.
But not at the expense of quality and breaking trust, you know.
Yeah.
Awesome.
Good.
But we'll love to, you know, like I said, like security is my dear and dear.
It's what I've spent most of the years of my career.
So anytime I can help a security company that's doing really cool stuff, that would be really cool.
Yeah.
I'll leave you with this.
You know, we talked about IAM very quickly, but also CISOs.
next next slide, or
Like we are so used to just grinding and creating credibility at the practitioner level that we haven't been able to uplift it yet.
And I know we've got a great story to tell.
So those would be our two sort of personas.
Yeah.
I also think there's some really cool creative things you can do.
I love your idea of like, hey, let's look from a perspective of an attacker, you know, and then reverse engineer and almost like write content as if you were the attacker explaining how.
very cool.
You know, like, and then that can be that persona, you know, like there's some cool  we can do.
I think that would be like really cool.
And then not hide that we're using AI as much, but really like, you know, look like, you know, yeah.
Very cool.
All right, guys.
Thanks so much for your time.
Appreciate it.
We'll follow up.
Good to meet you.
Take care.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Discuss potential collaboration between GrowthX and SpecterOps for AI-driven content creation and SEO optimization in the cybersecurity space.](https://fathom.video/share/AkXXeKYAzYuFsmqDG95bVYsAjUqxMLYH?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways
